coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots assisted exfoliation of atomically-thin 2D materials and as-formed 0D/2D van der Waals heterojunction for HER
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Jun
Zhang, Zheye
Zeng, Zhiping
Wang, Wenjun
Kong, Lingxuan
Liu, Jiyang
Chen, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Atomically-thin 2D materials have changed the landscapes of many fields. Their applications however are limited by lack of methods for readily and scalable production with high quality. Herein, a simple strategy is reported to exfoliate pristine single or few-layered 2D materials (MoS2, h-BN, WS2, g-C3N4 microsheets) using bottom-up grown amphiphilic graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as both the intercalation agent and dispersant. Further, it is shown that the as-formed GQD/MoS2 van der Waals heterojunctions (vdWHs) give enhanced performance for electrocatalysis of hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) owing to the synergistic coupling at the 0D/2D heterojunction, delivering a current density of 10 mA cm−2 at a low overpotential of 160 mV with a small Tafel slope of 56.9 mV dec−1. In addition to providing a new method for preparing ultrathin 2D microsheets, this study unleashes the application potential of 2D materials and GQD-based 0D/2D vdWHs as non-precious electrocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2D materials
Graphene quantum dots
van der Waals heterojunction
Hydrogen evolution reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Exfoliation of ultrathin 2D microsheets from bulk counterparts

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Agency for Science, Technology and Research

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by This work was supported by an AME-IRG grant ( A1983c0025 ) from Agency for Science, Technology and Research (A∗STAR) of Singapore and an AcRF tier 2 grant ( MOE2017-T2-2-005 ) from Ministry of Education (Singapore).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of GQD assisted exfoliation of MoS2, h-BN, WS2 and g-C3N4 microsheets. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM image of GQDs, with the inset showing a HRTEM image of a GQD with lattice spacing being resolved. (b) AFM image of GQDs with the inset showing the height profile along the red line. (c) Tyndall effect of the aqueous dispersions of the obtained 2D material microsheets. AFM and TEM images of the exfoliated (d, h) MoS2, (e, i) h-BN, (f, j) WS2, (g, k) g-C3N4 microsheets. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

XRD patterns and Raman spectra of (a, e) MoS2, (b, f) h-BN, (c, g) WS2, (d, h) g-C3N4 microsheets. Insets in (a–d) are enlarged (002) XRD peaks. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) AFM image of GQD/MoS2 with an inset showing the height profile along the red line. (b) TEM and (c) HRTEM images of GQD/MoS2. (d) Corresponding FFT patterns of MoS2 (green), GQD (orange) and GQD/MoS2 (red) processed from the HRTEM image of GQD/MoS2 microsheets. Scale bars = 5 1/nm. (e) Raman spectra of GQD/MoS2, GQDs and bulk MoS2. High-resolution XPS spectra of (f) Mo 3d and (g) S 2p of GQD/MoS2 and MoS2 microsheets. (h) UV–vis absorption spectrum and (i) PL spectra of GQD/MoS2 microsheets. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Polarization curves and (b) HER Tafel plots of bulk MoS2, MoS2 microsheets, GQD/MoS2, Pt/C in 0.5 M H2SO4. (c) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plots of bulk MoS2, MoS2 microsheets and GQD/MoS2. (d) Stability test of GQD/MoS2 and Pt/C. Inset schematically shows the probable electrocatalytic mechanism of GQD/MoS2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots assisted exfoliation of atomically-thin 2D materials and as-formed 0D/2D van der Waals heterojunction for HER

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, 928 Second Avenue, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, 928 Second Avenue, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Atomically-thin 2D materials have changed the landscapes of many fields. Their applications however are limited by lack of methods for readily and scalable production with high quality. Herein, a simple strategy is reported to exfoliate pristine single or few-layered 2D materials (MoS2, h-BN, WS2, g-C3N4 microsheets) using bottom-up grown amphiphilic graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as both the intercalation agent and dispersant. Further, it is shown that the as-formed GQD/MoS2 van der Waals heterojunctions (vdWHs) give enhanced performance for electrocatalysis of hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) owing to the synergistic coupling at the 0D/2D heterojunction, delivering a current density of 10 mA cm−2 at a low overpotential of 160 mV with a small Tafel slope of 56.9 mV dec−1. In addition to providing a new method for preparing ultrathin 2D microsheets, this study unleashes the application potential of 2D materials and GQD-based 0D/2D vdWHs as non-precious electrocatalysts.

We report a simple strategy to exfoliate pristine single or few-layered 2D materials (MoS2, h-BN, WS2, g-C3N4 microsheets) using bottom-up grown amphiphilic graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as both the intercalation agent and dispersant. The as-formed GQD/MoS2 van der Waals heterojunctions give enhanced electrocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution reaction owing to the synergistic coupling at the 0D/2D heterojunction.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Since mechanically exfoliated graphene being reported in 2004 [1], various atomically-thin two-dimensional (2D) materials have emerged and changed the landscape of many fields such as energy storage and conversion, catalysis, imaging and display, optoelectronics, thermoelectric device, and sensing technology, because their properties are extraordinary or complement with their bulk counterparts [2–8]. However, the applications of 2D materials are largely hindered by lack of methods for readily and scalable production with high quality.

Although chemical vapor deposition has been used to synthesize pristine 2D materials of wafer scale, it is of high cost and not amenable for mass production due to the low throughput and the need to be transferred from the growth substrate [9]. Currently, large-scale production mostly relies on chemical exfoliation from bulk materials, which however introduce chemical groups and defects into the resulting 2D sheets and has low yield percentage [10,11]. Alternatively, liquid-phase exfoliation (LPE) has been developed to exfoliate 2D materials without damaging their crystalline structures, for which high-power mechanical agitation (e.g., tip-sonication, high-shear mixing) is used to weaken van der Waals (vdW) interaction between the stacked atomic layers and organic solvent (e.g., N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone, N,N-dimethyl formamide, and dimethyl sulfoxide) is used to disperse the hydrophobic 2D materials [12–17]. But the efficiencies of these method are not satisfactory and cause environmental concern. In addition, the obtained hydrophobic 2D nanosheets are not suitable for aqueous processing [18].

Recently, amphiphilic dispersants, particularly pyrene derivatives, have been used to enhance the efficiency of LPE [19–23]. Pyrene core can intercalate into the layered structure and bind with the 2D materials via π-π, hydrophobic or vdW interactions while the attached chemical groups assist aqueous dispersion [24]. On the other hand, studies have suggested that the chemical groups compromise the interaction with 2D materials due to steric hindrance and charge transfer to the small carbon core [25–27]. Hence, we conceive that graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with functional groups at the edges, which in a way can be regarded as a giant amphiphilic polyaromatic molecule, could intercalate into and interact with 2D materials more effectively. Indeed, we have recently demonstrated readily aqueous exfoliation of pristine single-layered graphene sheets using GQDs hydrothermally grown from naphthalene [28]. In addition, various studies have demonstrated the novel properties and applications of the hybrids between GQDs and various 2D materials [29–31].

Herein, we present a general strategy to exfoliate atomically thin 2D materials (MoS2, h-BN, WS2, g-C3N4 microsheets) in water/ethanol mixture under low-power bath sonication, using GQDs grown from pyrene as both the intercalation agent and dispersant (Fig. 1 ). Furthermore, the as-formed GQD/MoS2 van der Waals heterojunctions (vdWHs) exhibit enhanced electrocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) owing to the synergistic coupling at such 0D/2D heterojunctions. The as-demonstrated scalable production of 2D materials sheets without chemical groups paves the way for their practical applications. This study also testifies the potential of GQD-based 0D/2D vdWHs.

All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and utilized without further purification, including pyrene, nitric acid, sodium hydroxide, MoS2 powder (6–40 μm), WS2 powder (∼2 μm), dicyandiamide, 100% ethanol, carbon black, Pt/C (20 wt% Pt on Vulcan XC-72R), Nafion (5 wt%). h-BN powder (8–10 μm) was purchased from Shandong Avant New Material Technology. g-C3N4 powder was synthesized by dicyandiamide as reported previously [32].

Pyrene (2.5 g) was added in 200 mL concentrated nitric acid and stirred at 80 °C for 13 h. Nitro-pyrene powder was obtained after filtering and drying at 60 °C. Then, 60 mg nitro-pyrene was dispersed in 30 mL NaOH (7.5 mM) and transferred to 50 mL Teflon autoclave for 8 h hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C. After dialysis (MWCO 500 Da) for 3 days, the remaining GQDs solution was filtered by 0.1 μm syringe filter to remove large particles and aggregates. Finally, GQD powder was obtained after thorough freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exfoliation of ultrathin 2D microsheets from bulk counterparts
ce:section.ce:para: 1.25 g of bulk powder and 25 mg GQD powder were dispersed in the 250 mL water/ethanol (Volume ratio = 1:1) mixture. After bath ultrasonication for 8 h, the dispersion was centrifuged at 2500 rpm to remove the unexfoliated bulk materials with the sediment retained for future resonication. The supernatant was then centrifuged at 13,500 rpm to remove free GQDs and the sediment retained. The sediment was redispersed in water and dialyzed overnight to fully remove ethanol and remaining free GQDs, followed by freeze-drying. To obtain hybrid sheets with abundant GQD/MoS2 heterojunctions, GQDs was increased to 250 mg for exfoliation.

The morphology of the samples was examined by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker ICON) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM 2100PLUS), field-emission SEM (FESEM, JEOL JSM-6700). Their chemical compositions were revealed by X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos AXIS Supra) using Al/Ag Kα X-ray monochromatic source. The UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence properties were characterized by a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) and a Fluorolog® PL spectrometer (Horiba), respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using a Bruker D2 Advance with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). Raman spectra were collected by a Renishaw InVia Reflex under 514 nm excitation.

Electrochemical Measurements were conducted with an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760D, Inc., Shanghai, China) with a rotating disk electrode at room temperature. The hydrogen evolution reactions were performed in N2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 solution using a three-electrode system with an Ag/AgCl electrode in saturated KCl solution as the reference and a graphite rod as the counter electrode. To prepare the working electrode, 10 mg catalyst and 2 mg carbon black were ultrasonicated in 1 mL solution containing 940 μL ethanol/water (volume ratio 1:1) and 60 μL Nafion. Then 10 μL catalyst ink was drop-casted on the surface of a freshly polished rotating disk electrode (5 mm in diameter). The loading mass of catalyst is 0.5 mg cm−2. All potentials are referenced to a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) following the equation: ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.059 pH + 0.197. Linear sweep voltammograms (LSVs) were obtained at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 with automatic 95% IR compensation. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried at an overpotential of −0.25 V vs. RHE in the frequency range of 0.1–100 kHz with an amplitude of 5 mV.

A modified method was used to prepare OH-functionalized GQDs based on a previous report [33]. Specifically, nitrated pyrene (trinitropyrene) molecules grow into GQDs through dehydrogenation in the presence of hydroxide (OH−), denitration, and molecular fusion via CC formation between activated carbon atoms under hydrothermal condition [28]. As the size of GQD increases, the energy barrier for dehydrogenation becomes too large to occur (thus subsequent molecular fusion) and the nitro groups are replaced by –OH groups at the edges.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) reveals that the produced GQDs are uniformly sized with an average diameter of 3.29 nm (±0.60 nm, n = 167) (Fig. S1a) and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to (001) facet of graphene (Fig. 2 a). Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) shows that the average thickness of GQDs is 1.17 nm (±0.38 nm, n = 123), indicating the single or double layered structure (Fig. 2b and Fig. S1b). The graphitic crystallinity of GQDs is also evidenced by the characteristic (002) peak of graphite in X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S2a). Raman spectrum presents an ordered G band at 1577 cm−1 and a weaker disordered D band at 1365 cm−1 (Fig. S2b), suggesting a graphitic structure with defects due to grafting of functional groups. In addition to the strong CC vibration at 1574 cm−1 in Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of GQD powders (Fig. S2c), a broad vibration of O–H bond at ∼3410 cm−1 and a vibration of C–OH at 1369 cm−1 appear, confirming the existence of –OH groups on GQDs. Consistently, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) gives C 1s (284 eV) and O 1s (532 eV) peaks and a weak signal from Na impurity (Fig. S2d). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum can be deconvolved into CC peak at 284.8 eV and another two peaks (C–O at 286.2 eV, O–CO at 288.8 eV) attributable to –OH groups conjugated at the edges of GQD (Fig. S2e), while the high-resolution O 1s spectrum shows the presence of O–H at 531.5 eV (Fig. S2f). UV–vis absorption of GQDs presents one obvious shoulder at ∼240 nm (5.2 eV) corresponding to π-π∗ transition of aromatic CC bonds in GQD basal plane (Fig. S3a). Comparing to semimetal like 2D graphene counterpart, the bandgap opens due to quantum confinement effect and thus GQD fluoresces. They give the maximum fluorescence emission at 577 nm under 500 nm excitation (Fig. S3b). Also, the emission peaks are essentially excitation independent, indicating that the size and properties of the obtained GQDs are highly homogeneous.

As shown schematically in Fig. 1, powders of the bulk materials were added to a water/ethanol solution (50 v/v%) containing 0.1 mg/mL GQDs and subjected to bath sonication (100 W) for 8 h. Ethanol helps to stably disperse the powders. Conceivably, sonication causes increase of the interlayer distance of layered bulk materials and renders GQDs kinetic energy, whereby allowing GQDs acting as “molecular wedges” to intercalate between the atomic layers which are weakly stacked via van der Waals force. The hydrophobic carbon plane of GQDs can intimately interact with the 2D materials, finally leading to their exfoliation into atomically thin microsheets which can remain stably dispersed due to the presence of the negatively charged –OH groups on GQDs. In comparison, due to “Magdeburger Halbkugeln” phenomenon arising from the vacuum condition between the layers, bath ultrasonication alone is not able to strip the bulk 2D materials [34]. Although tip sonication with high power density has been employed to exfoliate 2D materials, it is not possible for scalable production due to small active area, high energy cost, and heating issue [35].

The Tyndall effect of the as-obtained samples (MoS2, h-BN, WS2, g-C3N4 microsheets) dispersed in aqueous solution is presented in Fig. 2c, indicating their small size and good dispersibility. As uncovered by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), the exfoliated MoS2, h-BN, WS2, g-C3N4 microsheets exhibit thin thickness and lateral size of a few micrometers comparable to their powder precursors (Fig. S4). AFM images show that the thickness of MoS2, h-BN, WS2, g-C3N4 microsheets is ∼1.0, 0.8, 1.0 and 0.9 nm, all corresponding to single layer structures (Fig. 2d–g). The surfaces of these microsheets are rather clean due to low presence of GQDs which only infrequently cause quantized thickness increase of ∼1 nm (Fig. S5). As depicted in Fig. S6, the monolayer percentages of MoS2 and WS2 microsheets are >60% while most of h-BN and g-C3N4 microsheets are double or triple layered. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the four kinds of microsheets demonstrate their atomic thickness (Fig. 2h–k). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images clearly resolve the characteristic lattice spacings of 0.27 nm, 0.22 nm, 0.27 nm and 0.68 nm corresponding to (100) facet of MoS2, (101) facet of h-BN, (100) facet of WS2 and (100) facet of g-C3N4, respectively. It has been reported that bath sonication can exfoliate powders of 2D materials with heating (60 °C) and a prolonged duration (60 h). But the resulting 2D sheets have small lateral sizes (200–300 nm), a small portion of monolayers (<20%) and poor dispersibility [18]. In comparison, our method is more energy and time efficient, and able to produce pristine atomically-thin 2D sheets with a high yield and an order larger in lateral size.

As seen from the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 3 a–d), the exfoliated 2D microsheets exhibit the same characteristic peaks as their bulk counterparts, suggesting preservation of high crystallinity. But (002) peaks of the bulk precursors upshift with larger magnitudes due to stronger presence of layer-to-layer stacking [34,36]. Raman spectrum of MoS2 microsheets shows two prominent peaks at 377.9 (E1 2g) and 401.8 (A1g) cm−1, which are assigned to in-plane and out-plane Mo–S vibration of 2H–MoS2. The decreased gap between A1g peak and E1 2g peak compared with that of bulk MoS2 indicates that the layer number is less than 3 [37,38], which is consistent with the AFM observations (Fig. S6a). G band of h-BN microsheets upshifts 2 cm−1 to ∼1366 cm−1 as compared with that of bulk h-BN (Fig. 3f), indicating a higher in-plane strain and less interlayer interaction [39]. Fig. 3g shows that A1g (Γ, 401.8 cm−1) peak of WS2 microsheets downshifts compared with that of bulk WS2 (417.9 cm−1), resulting from less interlayer contributions to the phonon restoring forces [40]. Raman spectrum of bulk g-C3N4 shows a characteristic peak at 1149.4 cm−1 corresponding to the vibration modes of C–N heterocycles (Fig. 3h). A downshift of ∼6 cm−1 is observed from the exfoliated g-C3N4 microsheets, ascribable to the phonon confinement effect caused by reduced layer numbers [41]. In sum, the XRD and Raman characterizations confirm the successful exfoliation of atomically thin microsheets and their high crystallinity. Noteworthy, both XRD and Raman signals from GQDs are not observed due to their low presence on the surfaces of microsheets.

UV–vis absorption spectra of these microsheets dispersed in water/ethanol solution further confirm the successful exfoliation (Fig. S7). MoS2 microsheets present characteristic absorption peaks at 395 nm, 450 nm, 610 nm, 670 nm, suggesting the existence of layered 2H–MoS2 structure (Fig. S7a) [37]. The excitonic peaks at 610 nm (A) and 670 nm (B) arise from the K point of the Brillouin zone. The other two peaks are attributed to the direct transition from the deep valence band to the conduction band. No absorption peak but only exponential decay is observed in the absorption spectrum of h-BN microsheets due to the large bandgap as an insulating material (Fig. S7b) [42]. The successful exfoliation of WS2 microsheets is confirmed by the presence of absorption peak at 630 nm corresponding to direct bandgap absorption at K-point of the Brillouin zone (Fig. S7c) [43]. The absorption spectrum of g-C3N4 (Fig. S7d) shows two sharp peaks at 240 and 320 nm corresponding to π-π∗ transition of aromatic rings and n-π∗ transition introduced by electron transfer from a nitrogen nonbonding orbital to an aromatic anti-bonding orbital [44]. No absorption peaks from GQDs are noticeable in all samples indicating that the obtained 2D microsheets are nearly pure due to low presence of GQDs.

We have previously demonstrated GQD/graphene van der Waals heterojunction (vdWH) for photoelectrochemical water splitting using nitrogen and sulfur co-doped GQDs synthesized from naphthalene [28]. With doped GQDs serving as the catalytic center, the high performance is attributed to the synergistic cooperation between GQD and graphene at the Schottky-diode-like 0D/2D vdWH, specifically, low Schottky barrier for charge transfer, efficient separation of electron-hole pairs, the unique light adsorption and catalytical properties of GQDs, and high conductivity of graphene. This study demonstrates the great potential of interfacing GQDs with various 2D materials. Herein, we demonstrate the general feasibility of using GQDs as both intercalation agent and dispersant to exfoliate various layered materials, based on differently synthesized GQDs and 2D materials other than graphene. We also show that due to the weaker interaction between GQDs and other 2D materials comparing to the strong π-π interaction between GQDs and graphene, it is possible to produce nearly GQD-free pristine 2D material microsheets. To further testify the potential of GQD-based 0D/2D vdWHs as promising non-precious catalysts for various catalysis applications, we exfoliated MoS2 microsheets with GQD concentration being raised by 10 times (1 mg/mL) in order to produce MoS2 microsheets with abundant GQD/MoS2 vdWHs on the surface. In contrast to catalytically-insert pristine graphene, MoS2 can catalyze hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Hence, the performance of electrocatalytic HER based on GQD/MoS2 vdWHs with MoS2 as the catalytic center was investigated.

In Fig. 4 a, AFM image shows intermittent quantized increase of thickness, indicating that numerous GQDs are attached on the surface of a double-layered MoS2 microsheet to form GQD/MoS2 vdWHs. This is also confirmed by TEM (Fig. 4b). In the HRTEM image of a GQD/MoS2 microsheet (Fig. 4c), the resolved lattice spacings of 0.21 nm and 0.27 nm can be assigned to (100) facet of GQDs and (002) plane of MoS2 microsheets, respectively. The existence of two types of crystals and their lattice spacings were further confirmed by fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns of the HRTEM images (Fig. 4d). FFT pattern of MoS2 shows hexagonally arranged diffraction spots, revealing its highly crystallized hexagonal structure. FFT pattern of GQD only shows partial six-fold symmetry due to the low crystallinity in the presence of –OH groups, which is consistent with the previous report [45]. The FFT patterns from MoS2 and GQD are superimposed in their co-existed area. As seen, the diffraction spots of MoS2 are located closer to the center of the electron beam because MoS2 has a larger lattice spacing (0.27 nm) than that of GQD (0.21 nm). Raman spectrum (Fig. 4d) of GQD/MoS2 exhibits a G band upshift (∼15 cm−1) relative to that of GQD, suggesting electron transfer from GQD to MoS2 [46]. XPS survey spectrum of GQD/MoS2 shows the characteristic peaks from Mo, S, C, O as expected (Fig. S8a). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S8b) and O1s (Fig. S8c) are consistent with the spectrum from pure GQDs. The high-resolution Mo 3d spectrum of GQD/MoS2 reveals the characteristic peaks at 229.2 eV and 232.4 eV corresponding to 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 of Mo4+ of 2H–MoS2 (Fig. 4e). The high-resolution S 2p spectrum clearly depicts S 2p3/2 peak at 162.1 eV and S 2p1/2 peak at 163.3 eV, revealing −2 oxidation state of sulfur in GQD/MoS2 (Fig. 4f). Notably, Mo 3d peaks and S 2p peaks of GQD/MoS2 exhibit obvious downshift of ∼0.2 eV compared with that of MoS2 microsheets exfoliated with low concentration of GQDs (‘pure’ MoS2 microsheets), indicating the charge transfer from electron-rich GQDs to MoS2 [47,48]. But there is no obvious peak of GQDs observed in the XRD pattern of GQD/MoS2 because of low GQD to MoS2 mass ratio (Fig. S9).

As shown in Fig. 4h, the absorption spectrum of GQD/MoS2 microsheets are superimposition of those from GQDs and MoS2 microsheets [49]. Photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of “pure” MoS2 microsheets is not detectable using a PL spectrometer due to low quantum yield (data not shown). Interestingly, in addition to that from GQDs, two emission peaks at 685 nm (A exciton) and 627 nm (B exciton) appear in the PL spectrum of GQD/MoS2, arising from direct excitonic transitions at K point of the Brillion zone of MoS2 microsheets (Fig. 4i). These peaks are consistent with the PL peaks from MoS2 microsheets observed using high-power femtosecond pump–probe spectroscopy [50,51]. Apparently, the PL quantum yield of MoS2 microsheets significantly increases after being hybridized with GQDs. For A exciton, a significant upshift (Stokes shifts) is observed compared with the absorption peak of MoS2 microsheets at 670 nm (Fig. 4h), suggesting that the PL of MoS2 is dominated by trion (694 nm) which was enhanced by n-type doping of GQDs [51–53]. Taken together, MoS2 microsheets is decorated with GQDs with charge transfer occurring at the intimately coupled vdWHs.

Further, MoS2 microsheets, GQD/MoS2 microsheets, bulk MoS2 and commercial Pt/C were utilized as the catalysts for electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte. Catalysts were loaded on the surface of a rotating disk electrode (5 mm diameter) and linear sweep voltammograms (LSVs) were obtained at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 5 a, GQD/MoS2 sheet offers superior performance in comparison to bulk MoS2 and MoS2 microsheets. Specifically, GQD/MoS2 requires an overpotential (η10) of 160 mV to reach a current density of 10 mA cm−2, which is much small than those of bulk MoS2 (470 mV), MoS2 microsheets (340 mV). In addition, GQD/MoS2 exhibit a significantly smaller Tafel slope of 56.9 mV s−1 than bulk MoS2 (144.7 mV s−1) and MoS2 microsheets (93.6 mV s−1), which is closest to that of Pt/C (30.1 mV s−1), suggesting a fast reaction kinetics driven by Volmer-Heyrovsky-mechanism (Fig. 5b). The performance of GQD/MoS2 surpasses most 2H MoS2 nanosheets based catalysts reported recently (Table S1). As demonstrated in electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), GQD/MoS2 exhibits the smallest the charge transfer resistance (Fig. 5c), suggesting that the improved HER performance of GQD/MoS2 is originated from the fast charge transfer at the vdWH (inset of Fig. 5d). Charge transfer from GQDs to MoS2 introduces a valence-electron state of Mo, which desirably promotes proton adsorption during HER [47]. As another consequence, more negative oxidation state of S facilities catalysis of HER due to increase of unsaturated sulfide bonds [54]. In addition, the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) was measured by cyclic voltammogram to reflect the eﬀective electrochemically active surface area (Fig. S10). GQD/MoS2 displays the largest value of 5.65 mF cm−2 among the three MoS2 catalysts. Furthermore, compared with the commercial Pt/C catalyst, GQD/MoS2 is much more stable (Fig. 5d) and cheaper.

In summary, we demonstrate a simple and scalable method to exfoliate single or few-layered 2D material microsheets free of chemical groups, with assistance of low-power bath sonication and using bottom-up grown amphiphilic graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as both the intercalation agent and dispersant. Here, GQDs (∼3.29 nm) with –OH groups on the edges are hydrothermally synthesized using pyrene as the precursor. Depending on the concentration of GQDs, 2D microsheets with clean surface or with abundant as-formed 0D/2D van der Waals heterojunctions (vdWHs) on the surface can be obtained. The former is attractive for optoelectronic devices while the latter is appealing for catalysis. Although only pristine MoS2, h-BN, WS2, g-C3N4 microsheets are demonstrated here, the exfoliation of other 2D materials (e.g., MoSe2, and WSe2, black phosphorus) using this method may also be explored. As a proof-of-concept demonstration, the as-formed hybrid sheets with abundant GQD/MoS2 vdWHs were utilized for electrochemical catalysis of hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Compared with that of bulk MoS2 or MoS2 microsheets, the enhanced catalytic performance is attributed to the synergistic coupling at the vdWHs which is evidenced by Raman, XPS, PL, EIS characterizations. Many other methods are available to produce GQDs with a wide range of optoelectronic, physiochemical, or catalytic properties by introducing various chemical groups [55], heteroatom dopants [28] and defects [56]. Interfacing them with the family of 2D materials, various atomically-thin sheets with 0D/2D vdWHs can be obtained, promising novel applications in electrochemical and photo-catalysis, optoelectronics, energy conversion and storage.

J.G. designed and performed most of the experiments. Z. Zhang, Z. Zeng, L. K., W. W., J. L. contributed to materials characterizations and data interpretation. P.C. planned and supervised the project. J.G., Z. Zhang and P.C. wrote the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by This work was supported by an AME-IRG grant (A1983c0025) from Agency for Science, Technology and Research (A∗STAR) of Singapore and an AcRF tier 2 grant (MOE2017-T2-2-005) from Ministry of Education (Singapore).
